claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 13 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 13 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 15 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child (or children) you want to claim Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know the age of your child, and if you have multiple children, please tell me about each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and would like to know if I'm eligible.

I have two children I want to claim for. Alex is 13 and Blake is 15, so both are under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Great news - since both Alex (13) and Blake (15) are under 16 years old, they automatically meet the age qualification for Child Benefit.

Let me continue with the next step of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about the children's work status.

**Are both Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not working at all)?**

This is typically not an issue for children under 16, but I need to confirm this for both children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all. They're both still in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming that. Since neither Alex nor Blake are working, they meet this requirement. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

Specifically, this means they are not personally claiming benefits such as:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

This is quite rare for children their age, but I need to confirm - are both children free from receiving these types of benefits in their own name?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, neither of them receive any benefits in their own names. They're just regular kids living at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's confirmed. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are Alex and Blake currently in an apprenticeship?**

Since you mentioned they're both still in school, I'm assuming they are not in any apprenticeship programmes. Can you confirm that neither child is currently doing an apprenticeship?

(For reference: Most apprenticeships would disqualify a child from Child Benefit, except for Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales which are still approved.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are doing apprenticeships. They're both just in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming. Since neither Alex nor Blake are in apprenticeships, let's continue to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **You have pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. **You have pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. **You have pre-settled status** with sufficient financial resources (and not claiming certain income-based benefits)
6. **You have pre-settled status** and are a student with sufficient resources
7. **You have pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with the right to reside
8. **You're a Crown servant** living abroad (or in certain qualifying countries)

Do any of these apply to you? Most commonly, people simply live in the UK normally.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm based in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirements. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You qualify as responsible if **any one** of the following applies:

1. **You live with the children** - they live in your household
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** - you pay at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards their care (this includes food, clothes, pocket money, housing, gifts, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** - under a court order or agreement that covers the children's upkeep

Since you mentioned they're "regular kids living at home," can you confirm that both Alex and Blake live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex lives with me at home, yes. But Blake doesn't live with me - he's living elsewhere. I do help out financially with Blake though - I contribute towards his upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that important detail. So:

- **Alex** lives with you ✓
- **Blake** doesn't live with you, but you contribute financially towards his upkeep

For Blake, I need to confirm one thing:

**Is the amount you contribute towards Blake's upkeep at least equal to the Child Benefit amount (or equivalent in kind)?**

This contribution can include things like money for food, clothes, pocket money, housing costs, or gifts - and can be paid weekly, monthly, or as lump sums.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I help with his expenses regularly - clothes, food, that sort of thing. I pay about £26 a week towards his upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. £26 a week is a substantial contribution towards Blake's upkeep, which should meet the requirement of contributing at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards his care.

Since you're responsible for both children (Alex by living with you, Blake through your financial contributions), let me continue to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question about other claimants:**

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. 

**Is anyone else currently claiming (or planning to claim) Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Is there another parent or guardian claiming for either child?
- For Blake specifically, since he lives elsewhere - is the person he lives with claiming Child Benefit for him?

Please let me know the situation for both children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex - he lives with me.

For Blake, nobody else has priority to claim for him either. I'm the one supporting him financially.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming that. Since there are no other claimants for either Alex or Blake, we can proceed.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check the living situations for both children.

**Where are Alex and Blake currently living?**

For each child, please let me know if any of these special circumstances apply:

- **In hospital** - the child is currently staying in hospital
- **In care** - the child is in local authority care
- **Fostered** - the child is in a foster care arrangement
- **Normal living** - the child is living at home or in a normal living situation (not in hospital, care, or fostered)

You mentioned Alex lives with you at home. What about Blake - where is he living?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Alex is just living normally at home with me.

Blake has been in local authority care for a bit - just a couple of months now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex and Blake have different living situations, I'll need to check the rules for each of them.

For **Alex** (living normally at home), I'll proceed with the standard checks.

For **Blake** (in local authority care for a couple of months), there are specific rules I need to check regarding the duration and your ongoing involvement.

Let me first get the details on the care rules for Blake:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is an important question for **Blake**:

You mentioned he's been in local authority care for "a couple of months." The rules state that Child Benefit can continue for children in care for **up to 8 weeks**.

However, there are exceptions. Does **any** of the following apply to Blake?

1. **He has been in care for 8 weeks or less** (approximately 2 months could be just over 8 weeks)
2. **He spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you**
3. **He spends at least 2 consecutive nights per week at home**, OR
4. **He spends 7 or more consecutive days at home** at a time

Can you clarify:
- Exactly how long has Blake been in care? (Is it more or less than 8 weeks?)
- Does Blake come home to stay with you regularly - and if so, how often and for how long?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in care for about 4 weeks, so less than 8 weeks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Since Blake has been in care for only **4 weeks** (which is less than the 8-week limit), you can still claim Child Benefit for him.

Let me continue with the next check for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is the final check:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards the children's accommodation or maintenance?**

This question is particularly relevant for Blake since he's in local authority care. 

- For **Alex** (living at home with you): Is the council paying anything towards his accommodation or maintenance?
- For **Blake** (in care): Is the local council paying for his accommodation or maintenance while he's in care?

Generally, if the local council is paying for a child's accommodation or upkeep, you cannot receive Child Benefit for that child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Alex, no - the council isn't paying anything. He just lives at home with me.

For Blake, yes, the local authority is paying for his accommodation and maintenance while he's in care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important clarification. This means we have different outcomes for each child.

**For Alex:** The council is not paying anything, so he meets all the eligibility criteria. ✓

**For Blake:** Since the local authority is paying for his accommodation and maintenance while he's in care, unfortunately this affects eligibility for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Now I have all the information I need to provide you with the eligibility outcome for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Summary

### ✅ Alex (age 13) - **ELIGIBLE**

Great news! You **are eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Alex. He meets all the requirements:
- Under 16 years old
- Not working
- Not receiving benefits in his own right
- Not in an apprenticeship
- You live in the UK
- He lives with you (you're responsible for him)
- No one else is claiming for him
- The council is not paying for his accommodation/maintenance

### ❌ Blake (age 15) - **NOT ELIGIBLE**

Unfortunately, you **are not currently eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Blake. While he meets most of the criteria, the key issue is that the **local authority is paying for his accommodation and maintenance** while he's in care. This disqualifies eligibility regardless of your financial contributions.

---

## Next Steps

**For Alex**, you can claim Child Benefit by:
1. Applying online or by post using form CH2
2. Providing proof of your identity and Alex's birth certificate
3. Payments are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

**For Blake**, if his situation changes in the future - for example, if he returns home or the council stops paying for his accommodation - you may become eligible to claim for him at that point. You're welcome to reassess his eligibility if circumstances change.

**Note:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000, be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge, which may affect the amount you keep.

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about the assessment or the claims process?

reasonings: Alex (age 13) meets all eligibility criteria: He is under 16 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, not in an apprenticeship, you (the claimant) normally live in the UK, you are responsible for Alex as he lives with you, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him, he is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 15) does not meet all eligibility criteria. While he qualifies on most counts (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship, you live in the UK, you contribute to his upkeep, no competing claimants, and he has been in local authority care for less than 8 weeks), the fact that the local authority is paying for his accommodation and maintenance while he is in care makes him ineligible. You generally cannot receive Child Benefit for a child when the local council is paying towards their accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, who lives with you at home. However, you are not currently eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake because, although you contribute to his upkeep and he has been in care for less than 8 weeks, the local authority is paying for his accommodation and maintenance. If Blake's situation changes (e.g., he returns home or the council stops paying for his accommodation/maintenance), you may become eligible to claim for him at that point.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.reason: Local council is paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance
response.guidance: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. This applies to informal care arrangements and some fostering situations.

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 13) meets all eligibility criteria: He is under 16 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, not in an apprenticeship, you (the claimant) normally live in the UK, you are responsible for Alex as he lives with you, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him, he is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 15) does not meet all eligibility criteria. While he qualifies on most counts (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship, you live in the UK, you contribute to his upkeep, no competing claimants, and he has been in local authority care for less than 8 weeks), the fact that the local authority is paying for his accommodation and maintenance while he is in care makes him ineligible. You generally cannot receive Child Benefit for a child when the local council is paying towards their accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, who lives with you at home. However, you are not currently eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake because, although you contribute to his upkeep and he has been in care for less than 8 weeks, the local authority is paying for his accommodation and maintenance. If Blake's situation changes (e.g., he returns home or the council stops paying for his accommodation/maintenance), you may become eligible to claim for him at that point.
